title: Foreign aid given per capita vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Net](#dod:net-oda) [official development assistance (ODA)](#dod:oda), expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.
caption:
Foreign aid given per capita vs. GDP per capita
Net official development assistance (ODA), expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.

The scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and foreign aid contributions, plotting GDP per capita in 2021 international dollars on the horizontal axis against net ODA given per capita in constant 2023 US dollars on the vertical axis. Circles sized by population represent individual countries, color-coded by region: North America in red, Europe in blue, Asia in teal, and Oceania in light blue. The data shows a general positive trend where higher GDP per capita coincides with higher aid given per capita, though the distribution is uneven. European nations dominate the upper portion of the chart; Norway sits at the highest visible point with aid exceeding $800 per capita, followed by Luxembourg and Ireland near the $500 mark. Switzerland and Sweden also appear in the upper band, giving between $400 and $500 per person. Germany and the United Kingdom cluster in the middle range, contributing roughly $400 and $250 respectively. The United States, represented by the largest circle due to its population, sits lower on the vertical axis relative to its wealth, giving around $200 per capita despite a GDP per capita near $75,000. Qatar appears as an outlier on the far right with a GDP per capita over $110,000 but aid contributions around $250. Asian economies like Japan, South Korea, and Turkey cluster in the lower-left quadrant, with GDP per capita between $30,000 and $50,000 and aid contributions generally below $200. Thailand and Kazakhstan sit at the lowest end of both scales. The chart shows that while wealthier nations generally give more aid per person, the rate of contribution varies significantly across countries with similar economic output.
